page.content: The data presented in the table offers a comprehensive overview of patients diagnosed with R. equi infections, highlighting critical aspects of their clinical profiles and treatment outcomes. A notable aspect of the data is the onset of symptoms, which indicates that the majority of patients presented with common respiratory symptoms such as fever, cough, and dyspnea, with the earliest recorded onset in May 2005.

page.content: Among the cohort, a significant proportion of patients, 209 (71.3%), experienced these symptoms, underscoring the urgent nature of their medical condition. The table further elucidates the diagnostic techniques employed, revealing that lung infections were primarily identified through sputum cultures, chest X-rays, and high-resolution CT scans. Additionally, the immunological status of patients is reflected in the CD4+ cell counts, with an average of 133 cells/μl observed, alongside a viral load of 40,700 copies/ml of HIV-1 RNA. This information is crucial for understanding the interplay between HIV status and susceptibility to R. equi infections, informing both treatment strategies and patient management protocols.
